docs.title: About This Site
docs.text: SampleBlog is a place to share ideas, tutorials, and personal reflections. Built with MkDocs Material, it's fast, responsive, and fully accessible — with a light/dark mode toggle so you can read comfortably at any hour.

docs.title: What You'll Find Here
docs.text:
📝

Blog Posts

Regular articles covering technology, design, productivity, and more. New posts are added frequently — subscribe to stay up to date.

🎨

Beautiful Design

A modern gradient colour theme with full light and dark mode support, hero images on every page, and smooth animations throughout.

♿

Fully Accessible

WCAG 2.1 compliant — keyboard navigation, screen-reader friendly markup, skip links, sufficient colour contrast, and reduced-motion support.

📱

Responsive

Looks great on every device — from a wide desktop monitor down to a small mobile screen. Fluid typography and flexible layouts throughout.

☁️

Cloud Deployed

Hosted on GitHub Pages with a CI/CD pipeline and also configured for deployment to Azure Static Web Apps for enterprise-grade reliability.

🔍

Full-Text Search

Powerful client-side search across all pages and blog posts — no external service needed, works entirely in your browser.

docs.title: Latest Highlights
docs.text:
Check out some of the most recent posts:

  • Getting Started with MkDocs — A complete walkthrough of setting up MkDocs Material from scratch.
  • Building Responsive Layouts with CSS Grid — Tips and patterns for modern, flexible web layouts.
  • Accessibility Best Practices — Making the web inclusive for everyone.

View all posts →

docs.title: Other Ways to Connect
docs.text:
📧

Email

Prefer email? hello@sampleblog.example.com — we aim to reply within two business days.

🐙

GitHub

Found a bug or want to contribute? Open an issue or pull request on GitHub.

💬

Discussions

Join the conversation in GitHub Discussions — ask questions, share ideas, and connect with other readers.

Response Times

We do our best to respond to all messages within 2–3 business days. For urgent matters, please use the GitHub issue tracker.

docs.title: Accessibility Best Practices for the Modern Web
docs.text: The web is for everyone. Yet millions of people with disabilities encounter barriers daily. The good news: most accessibility improvements are simple to implement and benefit all users.

docs.title: Why Accessibility Matters
docs.text:
• ~16% of the world's population has some form of disability (WHO)
  • Screen readers, switch devices, and keyboard-only navigation are common tools
  • Accessible sites tend to rank better in search engines
  • Many countries have legal requirements (ADA, EN 301 549, etc.)
  • Accessibility improvements often improve UX for everyone

docs.title: The Four WCAG Principles (POUR)
docs.text:
The Web Content Accessibility Guidelines are organised around four principles:

Principle Meaning Perceivable Information must be presentable in ways users can perceive Operable Interface components must be operable by everyone Understandable Information and UI operation must be understandable Robust Content must be interpretable by assistive technologies

docs.title: 1. Semantic HTML
docs.text:
Use the right element for the job. Browsers and assistive technologies understand semantic HTML natively:

<!-- ❌ Bad -->
<div class="button" onclick="submit()">Submit</div>

<!-- ✅ Good -->
<button type="submit">Submit</button>

Use landmark elements: <header>, <nav>, <main>, <aside>, <footer>.

docs.title: 2. Colour Contrast
docs.text:
Text must have sufficient contrast against its background:

  • Normal text (< 18pt): minimum 4.5:1 ratio
  • Large text (≥ 18pt or 14pt bold): minimum 3:1 ratio
  • Use WebAIM Contrast Checker to verify

docs.title: 3. Keyboard Navigation
docs.text:
Every interactive element must be reachable and operable with a keyboard:

/* Never hide the focus ring entirely */
:focus-visible {
  outline: 3px solid #a855f7;
  outline-offset: 3px;
  border-radius: 3px;
}

Provide a skip link so keyboard users can jump past the navigation:

<a href="#main-content" class="skip-link">Skip to main content</a>

docs.title: 4. Alternative Text
docs.text:
Every image needs a text alternative:

<!-- Informative image -->
<img src="chart.png" alt="Bar chart showing 40% increase in Q4 sales">

<!-- Decorative image -->
<img src="divider.svg" alt="" aria-hidden="true">

docs.title: 5. Form Labels
docs.text:
Always associate labels with form controls:

<label for="email">Email address</label>
<input type="email" id="email" name="email" autocomplete="email">

Never use placeholder text as a substitute for a label.

docs.title: 6. ARIA — Use Sparingly
docs.text:
ARIA attributes fill gaps where semantic HTML isn't enough:

<!-- Live region for dynamic updates -->
<div role="alert" aria-live="polite" id="form-message"></div>

<!-- Progress indicator -->
<div role="progressbar" aria-valuenow="45" aria-valuemin="0" aria-valuemax="100">
  45%
</div>

First rule of ARIA

If you can use a native HTML element with the behaviour built in, do that instead of adding ARIA to a generic element.

docs.title: 7. Respecting User Preferences
docs.text:
/* Reduced motion */
@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) {
  *, *::before, *::after {
    animation-duration: 0.01ms !important;
    transition-duration: 0.01ms !important;
  }
}

/* High contrast mode */
@media (prefers-contrast: high) {
  .card {
    border: 2px solid currentColor;
  }
}

docs.title: Testing Your Site
docs.text:
1. Keyboard test — unplug your mouse and navigate with Tab/Enter/Space/Arrow keys
  2. Screen reader test — NVDA (Windows, free), VoiceOver (macOS/iOS, built-in), TalkBack (Android)
  3. Automated scan — axe DevTools, Lighthouse
  4. Colour contrast — WebAIM Contrast Checker
  5. User testing — involve people with disabilities in your process

docs.title: Further Resources
docs.text:
• WebAIM — comprehensive accessibility guidance
  • A11y Project — community-driven patterns
  • Inclusive Components — accessible component patterns

Accessibility is a journey, not a destination. Start small, test often, and keep improving. 🌏

docs.title: Getting Started with MkDocs
docs.text: MkDocs is a fast, simple, and beautiful static site generator that's perfect for building documentation sites and blogs. Combined with the Material theme, you get a professional-grade website with minimal effort.

docs.title: What is MkDocs?
docs.text: MkDocs is a Python-based static site generator that converts Markdown files into a fully functional website. It's widely used for documentation but works just as well as a blogging platform.

docs.title: Why Choose Material for MkDocs?
docs.text:
The Material theme provides:

  • 🎨 Beautiful design — clean, modern, and customisable
  • 🌙 Light/Dark mode — automatic or user-controlled
  • 📱 Responsive — great on every screen size
  • ♿ Accessible — WCAG compliant out of the box
  • 🔍 Full-text search — built-in, no external services needed
  • 📝 Blog plugin — first-class blogging support

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
First, make sure you have Python 3.8+ installed, then:

pip install mkdocs-material

docs.title: Creating Your First Site
docs.text:
mkdocs new my-site
cd my-site

This creates a minimal project structure:

my-site/
├── docs/
│   └── index.md
└── mkdocs.yml

docs.title: Configuring the Material Theme
docs.text:
Edit mkdocs.yml:

site_name: My Awesome Site

theme:
  name: material
  palette:
    - media: "(prefers-color-scheme: light)"
      scheme: default
      toggle:
        icon: material/weather-night
        name: Switch to dark mode
    - media: "(prefers-color-scheme: dark)"
      scheme: slate
      toggle:
        icon: material/weather-sunny
        name: Switch to light mode

docs.title: Adding the Blog Plugin
docs.text:
plugins:
  - search
  - blog:
      blog_dir: blog
      post_date_format: long
      categories: true
      authors: true

docs.title: Serving Locally
docs.text:
mkdocs serve

Open your browser at http://127.0.0.1:8000 and you're live!

docs.title: Deploying to GitHub Pages
docs.text:
mkdocs gh-deploy

That's it — MkDocs will build your site and push it to the gh-pages branch automatically.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Explore the Material reference for all available components
  • Add social cards for better link previews
  • Set up Google Analytics for traffic insights

Happy writing! ✨

docs.title: Building Responsive Layouts with CSS Grid
docs.text: CSS Grid transformed the way we build web layouts. Gone are the days of float hacks and clearfix — modern layouts are expressive, flexible, and easy to reason about.

docs.title: Why CSS Grid?
docs.text:
Before Grid, responsive layouts required complex combinations of floats, inline blocks, or Flexbox. CSS Grid gives us:

  • Two-dimensional control — rows AND columns at the same time
  • Intrinsic sizing — elements grow and shrink naturally
  • Fewer media queries — auto-fit and minmax() do the heavy lifting
  • Better readability — the layout intent is obvious in the code

docs.title: The Magic of auto-fit + minmax
docs.text:
This single line creates a fully responsive grid with no media queries:

.card-grid {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(280px, 1fr));
  gap: 1.5rem;
}

Cards will automatically wrap into a new row when they can no longer fit at their minimum width of 280px. The grid adapts from one column on mobile to as many columns as the container allows on larger screens.

docs.title: Named Grid Areas
docs.text:
For complex page layouts, named areas make the structure self-documenting:

.page-layout {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-areas:
    "header  header"
    "sidebar content"
    "footer  footer";
  grid-template-columns: 240px 1fr;
  grid-template-rows: auto 1fr auto;
  min-height: 100vh;
}

header  { grid-area: header;  }
aside   { grid-area: sidebar; }
main    { grid-area: content; }
footer  { grid-area: footer;  }

Rearranging the layout for mobile becomes trivial:

@media (max-width: 768px) {
  .page-layout {
    grid-template-areas:
      "header"
      "content"
      "sidebar"
      "footer";
    grid-template-columns: 1fr;
  }
}

docs.title: Intrinsic Typography with clamp()
docs.text:
Pair CSS Grid with clamp() for text that scales smoothly:

h1 {
  font-size: clamp(1.75rem, 5vw, 3.5rem);
  /* minimum: 1.75rem | preferred: 5vw | maximum: 3.5rem */
}

docs.title: A Complete Responsive Card Component
docs.text:
.feature-grid {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(260px, 1fr));
  gap: 1.5rem;
  padding: 2rem 0;
}

.feature-card {
  background: var(--surface);
  border-radius: 16px;
  padding: 1.75rem;
  box-shadow: 0 4px 24px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.08);
  transition: transform 0.25s ease, box-shadow 0.25s ease;
}

.feature-card:hover {
  transform: translateY(-4px);
  box-shadow: 0 8px 32px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.14);
}

docs.title: Browser Support
docs.text: CSS Grid is supported in all modern browsers — Chrome, Firefox, Safari, and Edge all have full support. You can use it confidently in production today.

docs.title: Further Reading
docs.text:
• MDN CSS Grid Layout
  • Grid by Example — a huge collection of patterns
  • CSS Grid Garden — learn by playing a game

Start experimenting — Grid makes what felt complex feel effortless. 🚀
